# *In situ* calibration of an extreme ultraviolet spectrometer for attosecond transient A3B2 show [pmg:line-break justify="yes"/]?absorption experiments

Xiaowei Wang, Michael Chini, Yan Cheng, Yi Wu, and Zenghu Chang

##### Author Affiliations

**Xiaowei Wang,1,2
Michael Chini,2
Yan Cheng,2
Yi Wu,2
and Zenghu Chang2,\***

*1Department of Physics, National University of Defense Technology, Changsha, Hunan 410073, China*

*2CREOL and Department of Physics, University of Central Florida, Orlando, Florida 32816, USA*

\*Corresponding author: [Zenghu.Chang@ucf.edu](mailto:Zenghu.Chang@ucf.edu)

Your library or personal account may give you access

![Add to BibSonomy](/images/bibsonomy-icon.png "BibSonomy")

## Abstract

We report a method for calibrating an extreme ultraviolet spectrometer based on a flat-field grazing incidence spherical grating in the energy range of 20–30 eV. By measuring absorption lines corresponding to singly excited states in helium atoms and autoionizing states in argon atoms, the photon energy of the detected light was determined. The spectral resolution of the spectrometer, 60 meV, was obtained by deconvolving the Fano resonance profile of argon autoionizing states from the measured absorption line profiles.

We report a method for calibrating an extreme ultraviolet spectrometer based on a flat-field grazing incidence spherical grating in the energy range of 20–30 eV. By measuring absorption lines corresponding to singly excited states in helium atoms and autoionizing states in argon atoms, the photon energy of the detected light was determined. The spectral resolution of the spectrometer, 60 meV, was obtained by deconvolving the Fano resonance profile of argon autoionizing states from the measured absorption line profiles.

enriched_points.expanded_content: In-situ setups enable real-time monitoring of samples under varying conditions, facilitating the study of dynamic processes without altering the sample environment.
Common equipment includes spectrometers, lasers, probes, and detectors, tailored for specific spectroscopic techniques like Raman, UV-Vis, and fluorescence.
Regular calibration ensures accuracy in measurements, often utilizing known standards while maintenance involves routine checks of equipment alignment and functionality.
